---
title: "C tier — respectable niche and methods-applied outlets"
---

These are legitimate venues — none of them should be confused with predatory or low-quality journals. They are here because they are either very specialized, oriented toward application over methodology, or have looser editorial standards than A/B tier.

## Statistical Methods in Medical Research

Applied biostatistics methodology, often serving as a "second-choice" venue for papers not placed at *Stats in Medicine* or *Biometrics*. Quality is variable.

## Journal of Clinical Epidemiology

Clinical epidemiology and evidence-based medicine methodology. Strong in its niche (systematic review methodology, reporting guidelines, risk prediction).

## Health Services Research

Health services and policy research. Relevant for health-policy-adjacent work (and plausibly for RSST-type evaluations), but the audience is primarily health services researchers rather than methodologists.

## Journal of Health Economics

The top field journal in health economics. If your work has an economic evaluation or behavioral economics angle.

## Lifetime Data Analysis

Specialized journal for survival and reliability analysis. Strong in its niche.

## Scandinavian Journal of Statistics

General methodology journal, somewhat theoretical, similar in scope to EJS but lower visibility.

## Econometric Theory

Theoretical econometrics, narrower scope than *Journal of Econometrics*.

## Computational Statistics and Data Analysis (CSDA)

Computational methodology. Papers emphasizing algorithms and implementation.

## JRSS-C (Applied Statistics)

The applied RSS journal. Heterogeneous in quality — some excellent papers, many routine applications.

## Biometrical Journal

German biometry journal; general biostatistics methodology. Respectable but lower-visibility than *Biometrics* or *Biostatistics*.
